title: Persistent Connections, Pipelining, Ordering, Reuse, and Closure · Independent Go Trial
summary: An independent protocol team receives different fragmentation, peer, trust, timing, and failure constraints and transfers persistent connections, pipelining, ordering, reuse, and closure into a new persistent-connection request and response state machine. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of persistent-connection request and response state machine with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, ca…
objectives: Determine HTTP/1.0 and HTTP/1.1 persistence from version, Connection options, framing completeness, errors, and local policy.
Consume, reject-and-close, or deliberately drain the current framed request before parsing a subsequent request.
Preserve request-response ordering across pipelined HTTP/1.1 messages even when application work finishes out of order.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Lab Persistence Defaults
steps.instruction:
lab case — A lab protocol team handles case 2171 in a signed webhook receiver behind an HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 gateway. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve HTTP/1.1 response order and reject reuse after incomplete or ambiguous input; preserve this operating constraint: the browser model cannot open a socket or claim packet-level proof; then saturate one stream window while another remains independently serviceable. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Determine HTTP/1.0 and HTTP/1.1 persistence from version, Connection options, framing completeness, errors, and local policy.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Lab Persistence Defaults.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for determine http/1.0 and http/1.1 persistence from version, connection options, framing completeness, errors, and local policy. For Commit to the mission model · Lab Persistence Defaults, keep Persistence Defaults connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

content.text: A lab protocol team handles case 2179 in a partner TLS endpoint negotiating HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 through ALPN. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve HTTP/1.1 response order and reject reuse after incomplete or ambiguous input; preserve this operating constraint: the maintained Go protocol stack remains the production transfer target; then block extra QPACK streams and fall back without removing HTTP/2 availability. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Close and discard connection state after ambiguous framing, parse failure, incomplete output, unexpected residual bytes, or state disagreement.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
